title: Lesson 7: When You Drift
description: You will stumble. Every Christian does. This lesson is not about guilt. It is about knowing how to turn around when you wander off the path, how to confess honestly, and how to trust the God who comes looking for you before you even know you are lost.

blocks.text: You have walked through six lessons now. You have learned what it means to follow Jesus, to be saved and baptized, to pray, to read the Bible, to be part of a church family, and to share your faith. This final lesson may be the one that holds all the others together. Because no matter how well you learn the foundations, there will be days when you drift. You will sin. You will wander. And when that happens, you need to know what to do next.
The Shepherd Who Comes Looking

Jesus told a story about a shepherd who had a hundred sheep. One wandered off, not in rebellion, not by any plan, just drifting one patch of grass at a time, until it looked up and realized it was alone.

What man of you, having a hundred sheep, if he has lost one of them, does not leave the ninety-nine in the open country, and go after the one that is lost, until he finds it? And when he has found it, he lays it on his shoulders, rejoicing. And when he comes home, he calls together his friends and his neighbors, saying to them, 'Rejoice with me, for I have found my sheep that was lost.' Just so, I tell you, there will be more joy in heaven over one sinner who repents than over ninety-nine righteous persons who need no repentance.

Notice what the shepherd does. He does not wait for the sheep to find its way back. He does not stand at the gate shaking his head. He leaves everything and goes after the one. And when he finds it, he does not scold it. He puts it on his shoulders and carries it home, and then he throws a party. That is the heart of God toward you. He is not angry when you wander. He comes looking. And there is more joy in heaven over your return than you can possibly imagine.
7.1 - Have you ever felt like the lost sheep, drifting without even realizing it? What did that look like?
What Repentance Really Means

Many people hear the word "repentance" and picture guilt, shame, or punishment. But the Bible paints a completely different picture. The Greek word for repentance is metanoia, and it means a complete change of mind. It is a 180-degree turn. You were walking one direction, away from God, and now you stop, turn around, and walk toward Him. That is it. Not groveling. Not earning your way back. Just turning.

Think about walking down a road. Every step away from God takes you further from His voice, His peace, and the life He designed for you. Sin always promises something good around the next bend, but the road just gets darker. Repentance is the moment you stop believing that lie, turn your whole self around, and start walking back toward the Father. And here is what makes it different from every other religion in the world: the moment you turn, you discover He was already running toward you.
Repent therefore, and turn back, that your sins may be blotted out, that times of refreshing may come from the presence of the Lord.

Did you catch that? Turning back does not lead to punishment, it leads to refreshing. Repentance is not the end of something good - it is the beginning of something better, the doorway back to peace, back to closeness with God, back to the life you were actually made for.
7.2 - What comes to mind when you hear the word "repentance"? Is it something you associate with guilt, or with freedom? Why?
Confession: Keeping the Air Clear

If repentance is the big turn, confession is the daily practice that keeps your heart clean. Confession simply means agreeing with God about what happened. You stop hiding, stop excusing, and stop pretending. You just say it out loud: "Lord, I was impatient with my family. I lied about that. I chose my comfort over obedience." When you name it honestly before God, something powerful happens. The weight lifts.
If we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness.

Read that promise slowly. He is faithful, meaning He will always do it. He is just, meaning the cross already paid the price, so forgiving you is not a favor, it is a finished transaction. And He does not just forgive, He cleanses. Not halfway. All of it. Every time you confess, God meets you with the full weight of the cross and wipes the slate clean.

Here is what a new believer needs to understand: confession does not change your standing with God. When you trusted Jesus, you became His child forever. That never changes. But unconfessed sin clouds the closeness you feel with Him. Think of it this way: a child who disobeys a parent is still their child, but there is tension in the home until things are made right. Confession clears the air and brings you back into the warmth of the Father's presence.
7.3 - Is there something you have been carrying around that you need to confess to God? What would it feel like to just name it honestly and let Him forgive it?
Conviction Is Not Condemnation

One of the most important things to understand as a new believer is the difference between two voices. Condemnation is the voice of the enemy. It says, "You are a failure. You will never change. Give up." It drives you away from God and into shame. Conviction is the voice of the Holy Spirit. It says, "You have sinned. Come home to the Father." It draws you back to God and into grace.

The difference is the direction they push you. Condemnation pushes you into hiding, isolation, and despair. Conviction pushes you toward honesty, confession, and the open arms of a God who already knows and still loves you. If what you are feeling makes you want to run from God, that is not His voice. If what you are feeling makes you want to run to God, that is the Holy Spirit doing exactly what He was sent to do.
There is therefore now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus.
7.4 - Think about a time you felt guilty about something. Was it more like condemnation (driving you away from God) or conviction (drawing you toward Him)? How can you tell the difference?
When You Stumble, Get Back Up

You will stumble, and every Christian does. A new believer needs to know this up front, because without that honesty, failure can feel like the end of the road when it is not. What defines you is not your failures but Jesus' faithfulness. The mark of a true follower of Jesus is not perfection, it is getting back up and walking with Him again.
Rejoice not over me, O my enemy; when I fall, I shall rise. When I sit in darkness, the Lord will be a light to me.

That is the promise. When you fall, you will rise. When you sit in darkness, the Lord Himself will be a light to you. Not because you are strong enough to recover, but because the Shepherd who left the ninety-nine is strong enough to carry you home.

In a future course, you will learn a practical tool called The Learning Circle that walks you through the rhythm of repentance step by step. But for now, here is the simplest version: when you drift, stop. Turn around. Tell God what happened. And receive the forgiveness He already secured for you at the cross. That is all repentance is. And it will keep your heart soft, your faith growing, and your walk with Jesus moving forward for the rest of your life.
Looking Back on the Journey

You have now walked through all seven lessons of the Starting in Discipleship Course. You have explored what it means to follow Jesus, to be saved and baptized, to pray, to read the Bible, to be part of the church, to share your faith, and to turn back to God when you drift. These are not just topics you have studied. They are the foundations of a life that is being transformed by the grace of God.

Create in me a clean heart, O God, and renew a right spirit within me. Cast me not away from your presence, and take not your Holy Spirit from me. Restore to me the joy of your salvation, and uphold me with a willing spirit.
7.6 - As you look back on all seven lessons, how has this study and reading the Gospel of John shaped your walk with God? What has changed in you?
Lesson 7 Review

Repentance is not a burden. It is a gift. A 180-degree turn back to a God who was already coming to find you. Confession keeps the air clear. And the Holy Spirit's conviction is not condemnation, it is love drawing you home. You have completed the Starting in Discipleship course. The next step is Growing Deep, where you will go deeper into your identity in Christ, explore the cross, and learn practical tools for walking with God through every season.

blocks.heading: Introduction to John 19, 20, and 21
blocks.intro: In John 19-21, you will read the most important events in all of human history: the crucifixion, death, and resurrection of Jesus Christ. Take your time with these chapters, because this is the foundation that everything you have studied in this course is built on.

blocks.text: Closing Reflections
What is one key thing you learned from this lesson, and what next step can you take this week?
What have you learned about God and yourself recently in your Daily Devotions?
What is a prayer request for this week?
